source.title: Metabolic engineering of Corynebacterium glutamicum for fatty alcohol production from glucose and wheat straw hydrolysate

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering Corynebacterium glutamicum for de novo fatty alcohol production from glucose and wheat straw hydrolysate by deregulating fatty acid biosynthesis, introducing fatty acyl-CoA reductase, attenuating thioesterase, enabling xylose utilization, and optimizing NADPH supply.
initial_request.known_context: C. glutamicum has not been engineered for fatty alcohol production, and its native fatty acid biosynthesis is tightly regulated by FasR.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: C. glutamicum has not been engineered for fatty alcohol production, and its native fatty acid biosynthesis is tightly regulated by FasR.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Tight regulation of fatty acid biosynthesis by FasR limits precursor supply for fatty alcohol production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Deleting fasR will derepress fatty acid biosynthesis and increase precursor supply for FAL production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Delete fasR to increase fatty acid precursor supply.
evolution_trajectory.observation: FA efflux observed only for ΔfasR mutant, secreting 72±42 mg FA/L under nitrogen-limiting conditions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Increased precursor supply from fasR deletion enables FAL production when FAR is expressed.

evidence.quote_or_span: the production of fatty alcohols (FAL) and derivates thereof with C. glutamicum has not been described so far
FA biosynthesis is tightly regulated by FasR
45 g urea L $^{-1}$ as the sole nitrogen source, FA efflux was observed only for the $\Delta fasR$ mutant which secreted $72 \pm 42$ mg $_{FA}$ L $^{-1}$ into the culture broth.

Upon cultivation in nitrogen-limiting NL-CgXII medium containing 1.45 g urea L $^{-1}$ as the sole nitrogen source, FA efflux was observed only for the $\Delta fasR$ mutant which secreted $72 \pm 42$ mg $_{FA}$ L $^{-1}$ into the culture broth.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ΔfasR mutant secretes fatty acids, providing precursor pool.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to convert fatty acyl-CoAs to fatty alcohols using a suitable fatty acyl-CoA reductase.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Heterologous expression of fatty acyl-CoA reductases (Maqu_2220 or Maqu_2507) in ΔfasR strain will produce FAL.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Clone maqu_2220 and maqu_2507 into pEKEx2 and express in ΔfasR mutant.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Maqu_2220 yields 482 mg/L FAL, outperforming Maqu_2507 (356 mg/L).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Genomic integration of maqu2220 drastically reduces FAL titer, motivating thioesterase attenuation.

evidence.quote_or_span: For that purpose, the genes encoding the reductases Maqu\_2220 and Maqu\_2507 were cloned into the plasmid pEKEx2 and expressed in the FA-producing $\Delta$ fasR mutant.

A Extra- and intracellular FAL concentrations obtained by $\Delta$ fasR mutants harboring pEKEx2-maqu2220 or pEKEx2-maqu2507; B distribution of 1-hexadecanol (C16:0), octadecanol (C18:0) and oleyl alcohol (C18:1) of the total FAL; C total FA produced by the FAR-expressing strains in comparison to WT and $\Delta$ fasR empty plasmid controls.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Genomic integration of maqu2220 drastically reduces FAL titer.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Thioesterase cg2692 may compete for acyl-CoA substrates, reducing FAL production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Attenuating thioesterase cg2692 by start codon exchange will reduce competition and increase FAL production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Exchange native ATG start codon of cg2692 to GTG or TTG.

evidence.quote_or_span: the native start codon ATG of cg2692 was exchanged for the less-preferred GTG and TTG codons
glutamicum $\Delta fasR$ cg2692 $_{TTG}$ (pEKEx2-maqu2220) reached a titer of 681 ± 27 mg $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ (Additional file 1: Figure S5) with a product yield and a volumetric productivity of 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: FAL production in ΔfasR cg2692_TTG (pEKEx2-maqu2220) under nitrogen-limited conditions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Nitrogen-limited medium may not be optimal for growth-coupled production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Switching to standard CgXII medium with PCA will improve FAL titer, yield, and productivity under growth-coupled conditions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Optimize medium to CgXII with PCA.
evolution_trajectory.observation: FAL titer improved to 544 mg/L, yield 0.054 Cmol/Cmol, productivity 0.109 g/L/h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: To use wheat straw hydrolysate, xylose utilization must be enabled.

evidence.quote_or_span: glutamicum $\Delta fasR$ cg2692 $_{TTG}$ (pEKEx2-maqu2220) produced within 15 h 544±20 mg $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ in CgXII medium supplemented with PCA which corresponds to a 33% increase of the total FAL titer, a 35% higher product yield (i.
02$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ from 20 g glucose L $^{-1}$ with a product yield of $0.054 \pm 0.
54 \pm 0.02$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ from 20 g glucose L $^{-1}$ with a product yield of $0.
09$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ with a product yield of $0.054 \pm 0.
final FAL titers of $2.45 \pm 0.09$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ and a volumetric productivity of $0.109 \pm 0.005$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ h $^{-1}$

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Growth on xylose may be poor due to low expression or activity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Initial xylose growth rate will be low.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Initial xylose growth is very poor (μmax 0.03 h⁻¹).

evidence.quote_or_span: ALE improved the growth rate by 200% from 0.03 to 0.11 h $^{-1}$

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Growth rate on xylose needs improvement for efficient utilization.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) will select for mutations that improve xylose growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ALE on xylose minimal medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ALE improves xylose growth rate to 0.11 h⁻¹; genome sequencing reveals a 53 bp duplication creating an additional RBS upstream of xylA, increasing mRNA 80-fold and enzyme activity 4-fold.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The evolved xylose module is transferred to the FAL production strain, but yield decreases.

evidence.quote_or_span: ALE improved the growth rate by 200% from 0.03 to 0.11 h $^{-1}$
the duplicated region in gX includes the RBS of the originally encoded gene actA and resulted in the occurrence of two RBS at a distance of 53 bp, suggesting boosted translation

This rearrangement duplicated a region spanning 21 bp of the UTR sequence and the first 32 bp of the xylA open reading frame and resulted in two occurrences of a predicted RBS (AGGAG) at 6 and 59 bp upstream of the start codon of xylA (Additional file 1: Figure S4B).
glutamicum gX were about 80-fold and 28-fold higher than in the parental strain C.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved gX strain grows well on xylose.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Transferring gX module to FAL production strain may not improve yield on hydrolysate due to metabolic imbalances.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Enabling xylose utilization will improve FAL production on wheat straw hydrolysate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Transfer gX module to FAL production strain and test on hydrolysate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Yield decreases by 18% to 0.058 Cmol/Cmol, likely due to NADPH limitation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: NADPH limitation hypothesis leads to pntAB overexpression design.

evidence.quote_or_span: neither product yield nor volumetric productivity were improved by enabling xylose utilization
Buschke et al. [12] reported insufficient NADPH supply through the oxidative pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) of a modified C. glutamicum strain cultivated on xylose
While the titer of 705 ± 77 mg $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ remained comparable to the control strain, the product yield decreased by 18% to

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Yield decrease on hydrolysate attributed to NADPH limitation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: NADPH supply may be insufficient for FAL production from xylose.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Overexpressing E. coli transhydrogenase pntAB will improve NADPH supply in xylose-utilizing strain.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: pntAB overexpression is tested and improves yield and productivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: the E. coli membrane-bound transhydrogenase-encoding genes pntAB were genomically integrated into the landing pad CgL12

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Effect of pntAB overexpression on FAL production from hydrolysate needs validation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: pntAB overexpression will increase yield and productivity in gX strain on hydrolysate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test FAL production in gX strain with pntAB on wheat straw hydrolysate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: pntAB overexpression increases yield by 29% to 0.075 Cmol/Cmol and productivity by 32% to 62 mg/L/h in gX strain on hydrolysate; no effect in control strain.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Improved strain is used in fed-batch cultivation to achieve high titer.

evidence.quote_or_span: The genomic integration of the transhydrogenase increased the volumetric productivity by 32% to 62±1 mg $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ h $^{-1}$ with the gX strain background and increased the product yield by 29% to 0.075±0.

The genomic integration of the transhydrogenase increased the volumetric productivity by 32% to 62±1 mg $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ h $^{-1}$ with the gX strain background and increased the product yield by 29% to 0.
When expressing the NADPH-regenerating E. coli transhydrogenase PntAB titers, yields, and volumetric productivities were improved in xylose-utilizing strains.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Shake flask production with pntAB improved yield and productivity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Fed-batch cultivation may further increase titer and productivity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fed-batch cultivation with wheat straw hydrolysate will achieve higher FAL titer and productivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform fed-batch cultivation with wheat straw hydrolysate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Fed-batch cultivation achieves 2.45 g/L FAL and 0.109 g/L/h productivity, a threefold increase over shake flasks.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Fed-batch reveals limitations like sequential sugar use and foaming.

evidence.quote_or_span: represent a more than threefold and 1.8-fold increase compared to the shaking flask experiments
final FAL titers of $2.45 \pm 0.09$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ and a volumetric productivity of $0.109 \pm 0.005$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ h $^{-1}$
005$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ h $^{-1}$ (Table 3), which represent a more than threefold and 1.8-fold increase compared to the shaking flask experiments, respectively (Table 2).

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sequential sugar utilization and foaming limit productivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze sugar consumption and process issues.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sequential sugar utilization observed: xylose consumed only after glucose depletion, limiting productivity; foaming issue during fed-batch.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Sequential sugar use indicates xylose utilization is still limiting.

evidence.quote_or_span: HPLC data still suggested that product formation was primarily based on carbon provided by glucose, indicating that the precursor supply for FAL synthesis might be limiting when growing on xylose.
Manual addition of the antifoaming agent was done aseptically when required.
final FAL titers of $2.45 \pm 0.09$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ and a volumetric productivity of $0.109 \pm 0.005$ g $_{FAL}$ L $^{-1}$ h $^{-1}$

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Xylose utilization still limited despite ALE and pntAB overexpression.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Xylose uptake is a limiting factor for efficient utilization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Identify xylose uptake as a target for further improvement.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Xylose utilization still limited; further improvements possible via transporter engineering or additional ALE.

evidence.quote_or_span: Xylose uptake is known as a limiting factor for the efficient utilization of this sugar
improving xylose uptake in C. glutamicum gX seems to be a straightforward approach to advance the growth properties further

validation_methods: HPLC analysis of FAL and sugars
success_verification.final_conclusion: Engineered C. glutamicum strain achieved 2.45 g/L FAL from wheat straw hydrolysate in fed-batch, with growth-coupled production and improved NADPH supply, though xylose utilization remains limiting.
limitations: Sequential sugar utilization (xylose consumed only after glucose depletion)
